elements.sent-2411-001: Guarded Kleene Algebra with Tests (GKAT) provides a sound and complete framework to reason about trace equivalence between simple imperative programs.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, there are still several notable limitations.
elements.sent-2411-003: First, GKAT is completely agnostic with respect to the meaning of primitives, to keep equivalence decidable.
elements.sent-2411-004: Second, GKAT excludes non-local control flow such as \command{goto , \command{break , and \command{return .}}}
elements.sent-2411-005: To overcome these limitations, we introduce Control-Flow GKAT (CF-GKAT), a system that allows reasoning about programs that include non-local control flow as well as hardcoded values.
elements.sent-2411-006: CF-GKAT is able to soundly and completely verify trace equivalence of a larger class of programs, while preserving the nearly-linear efficiency of GKAT.
elements.sent-2411-007: This makes CF-GKAT suitable for the verification of control-flow manipulating procedures, such as decompilation and \command{goto -elimination.}
elements.sent-2411-008: To demonstrate CF-GKAT's abilities, we validated the output of several highly non-trivial program transformations, such as Erosa and Hendren's \command{goto -elimination procedure and the output of Ghidra decompiler.}
elements.sent-2411-009: CF-GKAT opens up the application of Kleene Algebra to a wider set of challenges, and provides an important verification tool that can be applied to the field of decompilation and control-flow transformation.
elements.sent-2411-010: There are many notions of program equivalence with different granularity and computational complexity.
elements.sent-2411-011: At one end of the spectrum, syntactic equality compares two programs solely based on its syntax.
elements.sent-2411-012: Although decidable, this technique will not equate programs like the following: \begin{mathpar \comITE{t {p {q \and \comITE{ \neg t {q {p \end{mathpar Conversely, input-output equivalence relates two programs if and only if they yield the same output on the same input.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: This equivalence is very powerful, but also well-known to be undecidable.
elements.sent-2411-014: Situated between these two extremes, Guarded Kleene Algebra with Tests~\cite{kozen_BohmJacopiniTheorem_2008a,smolka_GuardedKleeneAlgebra_2020 , or GKAT for short, reasons about trace equivalence between simple \command{while -programs.}}
elements.sent-2411-015: By abstracting the meaning of the primitive tests and actions, it can focus on how tests determine which actions are performed.
elements.sent-2411-016: For example, the two programs above are equivalent in GKAT:\@ they both execute $p$ when $t$ holds, and $q$ when it does not.
elements.sent-2411-017: GKAT is also able to validate nontrivial equivalences like \begin{equation}\comWhile{t}{p};\ \comWhile{s}{\{\ q;\ \comWhile{t}{p}\ \}} \equiv \comWhile{t \lor s}{\{\ \comITE{t}{p}{q}\ \}}\end{equation} Surprisingly, GKAT equivalence is decidable in nearly-linear time (assuming the set of test variables is fixed)~\cite{smolka_GuardedKleeneAlgebra_2020 , making it a reasonable compromise between complexity and granularity.}
elements.sent-2411-018: Nevertheless, the abstraction of GKAT comes at the price of not being able to validate some straightforward equivalences.
elements.sent-2411-019: First, as mentioned, GKAT disregards the meaning of primitive programs and tests.
elements.sent-2411-020: For instance, when given a program like \begin{equation}\comITE{y \neq 0}{\{\ x := 42;\ p\ \}}{\{\ x := 42;\ q\ \}}, \label[prog]{prog: assignment inside branches}\end{equation} we can note that a change in the value of $x$ does not have effect on whether $y \neq 0$.
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\comITE{y \neq 0}{\{\ x := 42;\ p\ \}}{\{\ x := 42;\ q\ \}}, \label[prog]{prog: assignment inside branches}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-021: Hence, it should be possible to factor the assignment to $x$ out of the branches, and obtain \begin{equation}x := 42;\ \comITE{y \neq 0}{p}{q}. \label[prog]{prog: assignment outside branches}\end{equation} GKAT does not admit this equivalence, precisely because it is agnostic with respect to the meaning of primitive actions.
elements.sent-2411-022: However, moving to a setting that accounts for the semantics of actions is hard, because Turing completeness --- and by extension, undecidability --- lurks nearby~\cite{kozen_KleeneAlgebraTests_1996, kuznetsov_ComplexityReasoningKleene_2023,azevedodeamorim_KleeneAlgebraCommutativity_2024 Second, GKAT excludes non-local control-flow constructs like \(\command{goto}\), \(\comBrk\), and \(\comRet\).}
elements.sent-2411-023: In a general imperative language, this does not limit expressivity, as these constructs can be recovered using variables~\cite{erosa-hendren-1994 --- indeed, the Böhm-Jacopini theorem says that every type of control flow can be written using a single $while$-loop~\cite{DBLP:journals/cacm/BohmJ66 .}}
elements.sent-2411-024: However, lacking both variables and non-local control structures, GKAT is not able to express all control flow in real-world programs.
elements.sent-2411-025: As a concrete example, consider the programs below.
elements.sent-2411-026: The control flow in \Cref{prog: goto version two state automaton is based purely on labels and $goto$.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Meanwhile, \Cref{prog: break version two state automaton is structured as a loop with the option to terminate early using $\comBrk$.}
elements.sent-2411-028: These programs happen to be trace equivalent (i.e., they always execute the same actions in the same order) but represent behavior not expressible in plain GKAT~\cite{schmid_GuardedKleeneAlgebra_2021 . \begin{equation} \comLabel{ \ell _{0}};\ \comIT{\neg t}{\comGoto{ \ell _{1}}};\ p;\ \comIT{t}{\comGoto{ \ell _{1}}};\ q;\ \comGoto{ \ell _{0}};\ \comLabel{ \ell _{1}} \label[prog]{prog: goto version two state automaton} [3pt] \comWhile{t}{\{\ p;\ \comITE{ \neg t}{q}{\comBrk}\ \}} \label[prog]{prog: break version two state automaton}\end{equation} As it turns out, deciding equivalence between programs such as these is essential in validating control-flow manipulation procedures.}
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation} \comLabel{ \ell _{0}};\ \comIT{\neg t}{\comGoto{ \ell _{1}}};\ p;\ \comIT{t}{\comGoto{ \ell _{1}}};\ q;\ \comGoto{ \ell _{0}};\ \comLabel{ \ell _{1}} \label[prog]{prog: goto version two state automaton} [3pt] \comWhile{t}{\{\ p;\ \comITE{ \neg t}{q}{\comBrk}\ \}} \label[prog]{prog: break version two state automaton}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-029: Specifically, consider the control flow structuring phase of a decompiler~\cite{cifuentes-1994 , which is tasked with converting conditional and unconditional jumps into more conventional control flow constructs.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Thus, the control-flow structuring process is correct when \Cref{prog: goto version two state automaton,prog: break version two state automaton are equivalent.}
elements.sent-2411-031: To overcome the limitations of GKAT, we propose control-flow GKAT (CF-GKAT), an extension that is capable of equating some interesting programs.
elements.sent-2411-032: Concretely, we extend GKAT in two ways.
elements.sent-2411-033: First, we add indicator variables, which can be assigned and tested against hardcoded values, and do not appear in other primitive actions and tests.
elements.sent-2411-034: For example, assignments like $x := 42$ are allowed, but assignments like $x := y + 1$ are not.
elements.sent-2411-035: This strikes a delicate balance, by being weak enough to exclude general computation (thus keeping equivalence decidable), yet strong enough to model the equivalence of \Cref{prog: assignment inside branches,prog: assignment outside branches .}
elements.sent-2411-036: The addition of indicator variables empowers CF-GKAT to validate control-flow transformation algorithms that use them~\cite{yakdan_NoMoreGotos_2015,erosa-hendren-1994 .}
elements.sent-2411-037: We focus on a single indicator variable for brevity; an extension to multiple indicator variables should be straightforward.
elements.sent-2411-038: Second, we extend GKAT with the non-local control-flow constructs \(\comGoto{\!}\), \(\comBrk\) and \(\comRet\).
elements.sent-2411-039: This poses a challenge, as the non-local nature of these commands prevents a compositional semantics --- after all, the precise meaning of a statement like \(\comBrk\) depends on its context.
elements.sent-2411-040: To overcome this, we propose an intermediate continuation semantics.
elements.sent-2411-041: In this semantics, each trace is tagged with a ``continuation'' that can accept (terminate normally), break, return, or go to a label.
elements.sent-2411-042: Then, the trace semantics of the program can be obtained by resolving these continuations.
elements.sent-2411-043: We were able to design an automaton model for CF-GKAT, where every CF-GKAT expression can be converted into a CF-GKAT automaton while preserving the continuation semantics.
elements.sent-2411-044: Furthermore, CF-GKAT automata and continuation semantics can be lowered into GKAT automata and trace semantics respectively, while preserving their semantic correspondence.
elements.sent-2411-045: We are thus able to reduce the problem of deciding trace equivalence of programs to deciding bisimilarity of two GKAT automata, which can be done efficiently.
elements.sent-2411-046: Consequently, CF-GKAT can soundly and completely validate trace equivalence of a larger class of programs, while preserving the nearly-linear efficiency of GKAT\@.
elements.sent-2411-047: For instance, it can automatically validate that \Cref{prog: break version two state automaton,prog: goto version two state automaton are equivalent to each other, and also to their single-loop equivalent obtained via the Böhm-Jacopini theorem~\cite{DBLP:journals/cacm/BohmJ66 : \begin{equation} x := 1;\ \comWhile{x \neq 0}{\{ \qquad \comITE{x = 1 \land t}{\{\ p;\ x := 2\ \} \qquad\qquad}{\comITE{x = 2 \land \neg t} {\{\ q;\ x := 1\ \} \qquad\qquad}{x := 0} }\ \}}  \label[prog]{prog: indicator version two state automaton}\end{equation} To put this theory to work, we implemented an equivalence checker for CF-GKAT\@, with a front-end that can convert C code to CF-GKAT expressions by leveraging Clang's parser.}}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation} x := 1;\ \comWhile{x \neq 0}{\{ \qquad \comITE{x = 1 \land t}{\{\ p;\ x := 2\ \} \qquad\qquad}{\comITE{x = 2 \land \neg t} {\{\ q;\ x := 1\ \} \qquad\qquad}{x := 0} }\ \}}  \label[prog]{prog: indicator version two state automaton}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-048: The resulting tool is able to automatically validate highly non-trivial program transformations like Erosa and Hendren's classic \command{goto -elimination procedure~\cite{erosa-hendren-1994 and the control flow structuring of Ghidra.\footnote{\url{https://ghidra-sre.org/.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: The remainder of this article is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-050: In \Cref{sec:overview , we give an overview of CF-GKAT, including its syntax, continuation semantics, and trace semantics.}
elements.sent-2411-051: In \Cref{section:decision procedure , we propose an automaton model for the continuation semantics, called CF-GKAT automata; we show how to translate a CF-GKAT expression to a CF-GKAT automaton, which in turn can be lowered to a GKAT automaton; ultimately, this gives rise to an algorithm for checking trace equivalence of CF-GKAT expressions.}
elements.sent-2411-052: In \Cref{sec:experiments , we report on an implementation of our algorithm, along with several experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-053: We discuss related work in \Cref{sec:related-work , and conclude in \Cref{sec:conclusion .}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Our proofs are formalized~\cite{artifact in Coq~\cite{coq-web ; we provide proof sketches for the sake of intuition.}}
elements.sent-2411-055: In this section, we introduce the language of CF-GKAT, and gradually develop its semantics.
elements.sent-2411-056: We begin by explaining the syntax of CF-GKAT;\@ after that, we delve into the semantics of its tests.
elements.sent-2411-057: We then introduce the intermediate semantic model of (labeled and indexed families of) guarded languages with continuations, which is flattened into to a model based on guarded languages.
elements.sent-2411-058: Having defined these tools, we then conclude by giving a semantics to CF-GKAT programs in this model.
elements.sent-2411-059: Along the way, we will single out and explain some of the finer points using examples.
elements.sent-2411-060: The syntax of CF-GKAT consists of two levels, similar to GKAT\@.
elements.sent-2411-061: At the bottom level, there are tests.
elements.sent-2411-062: These are Boolean assertions that can occur as guards inside conditional statements, or within assertions that occur in the program text.
elements.sent-2411-063: To model them, we fix a finite set of primitive tests $T$, which represent uninterpreted expressions that may or may not hold.
elements.sent-2411-064: The full syntax is as follows. \begin{equation}\BExp \ni b, c ::= \false \mid \true \mid t \in T \mid {\color{blue}x = i\ (i \in I)} \mid b \lor c \mid b \land c \mid \neg b\end{equation} Compared to GKAT, tests in CF-GKAT include the indicator variable test $x = i$ (highlighted in \textcolor{blue {blue ) for each indicator value $i$ drawn from a finite but fixed set of possible indicator values $I$.}}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}\BExp \ni b, c ::= \false \mid \true \mid t \in T \mid {\color{blue}x = i\ (i \in I)} \mid b \lor c \mid b \land c \mid \neg b\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-065: As the notation suggests, this test holds when the indicator variable $x$ currently has the value $i$.
elements.sent-2411-066: The top level syntax of GKAT is built using a finite set of uninterpreted commands \( \Sigma \) (the primitive actions), as well as assertions of the form $\comAssert{b}$, where $b \in \BExp$ is a test.
elements.sent-2411-067: Expressions are composed using sequencing, if statements, and while loops.
elements.sent-2411-068: CF-GKAT extends the base elements of the syntax with indicator variable assignments \(x := i\) (for each $i \in I$), which changes the value of the indicator variable \(x\) to \(i\).
elements.sent-2411-069: In addition, it adds the non-local control flow commands $\comBrk$ and $\comRet$, as well as $\comGoto{\ell}$ and $\comLabel{\ell}$, where $\ell$ is taken from a fixed but finite set of labels $L$.
elements.sent-2411-070: The full syntax is given below (additions relative to GKAT highlighted in \textcolor{blue {blue again). \begin{equation}\Exp \ni e, f ::= {} \comAssert{b} \mid p \in \Sigma \mid {\color{blue}x := i\ (i \in I)} \mid e; f \mid \comITE{b}{e}{f} \mid {} \comWhile{b}{e} \mid {\color{blue} \comBrk} \mid {\color{blue} \comRet} \mid {\color{blue} \comGoto{ \ell }\ ( \ell \in L)} \mid {\color{blue} \comLabel{ \ell }\ ( \ell \in L)}\end{equation} A valid program, or program for short, is an expression without (1)~duplicate labels, (2)~$\command{goto}$ commands with an undefined label, or (3)~$\comBrk$ statements that occur outside a loop.}}
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}\Exp \ni e, f ::= {} \comAssert{b} \mid p \in \Sigma \mid {\color{blue}x := i\ (i \in I)} \mid e; f \mid \comITE{b}{e}{f} \mid {} \comWhile{b}{e} \mid {\color{blue} \comBrk} \mid {\color{blue} \comRet} \mid {\color{blue} \comGoto{ \ell }\ ( \ell \in L)} \mid {\color{blue} \comLabel{ \ell }\ ( \ell \in L)}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-071: For the sake of simplicity, we assume that the reader does not require a more formal definition of this notion.
elements.sent-2411-072: Any GKAT expression is a valid program.
elements.sent-2411-073: Also, \cref{prog: break version two state automaton, prog: goto version two state automaton, prog: indicator version two state automaton from the introduction are all valid CF-GKAT programs.}
elements.sent-2411-074: The following expressions are not valid programs: \begin{equation}\comLabel{ \ell }; (\comITE{t}{\comLabel{ \ell }; p}{q}) \tag{the label \( \ell \) is defined twice} (\comWhile{\true}{p}); \comGoto{ \ell } \tag{the label \( \ell \) is undefined} \comITE{t}{\comBrk}{p} \tag{$\comBrk$ appears outside a loop}\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}\comLabel{ \ell }; (\comITE{t}{\comLabel{ \ell }; p}{q}) \tag{the label \( \ell \) is defined twice} (\comWhile{\true}{p}); \comGoto{ \ell } \tag{the label \( \ell \) is undefined} \comITE{t}{\comBrk}{p} \tag{$\comBrk$ appears outside a loop}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-075: To assign a semantics to CF-GKAT expressions, we first need to talk about the semantics of tests.
elements.sent-2411-076: Intuitively, each test in a CF-GKAT expression symbolically denotes a set of execution contexts in which it is true.
elements.sent-2411-077: But how do we model an execution context? Because the primitive tests from $T$ are uninterpreted, we represent them by simply listing the ones that are true; the unlisted tests are then assumed to be false.
elements.sent-2411-078: This is in line with how the semantics of (G)KAT handles tests~\cite{smolka_GuardedKleeneAlgebra_2020,kozen_KleeneAlgebraTests_1997 .}
elements.sent-2411-079: As for the indicator tests, we include the current value of the indicator variable $x$ in the execution context.
elements.sent-2411-080: This means that each execution context is of the form $(i, \alpha )$, for $i \in I$ and $ \alpha \subseteq T$.
elements.sent-2411-081: Putting these ideas together, we can calculate the set of execution contexts that satisfy a given test \(b \in \BExp\) by induction.
elements.sent-2411-082: This set will be regarded as the semantics of \(b\).
elements.sent-2411-083: Let $\At$ denote $2^T$, the set of atoms of (the free Boolean algebra generated by) $T$.
elements.sent-2411-084: We define the Boolean semantics function $ \lBrack - \rBrack : \BExp \to 2^{I \times \At}$ inductively, as follows. \begin{equation}\lBrack \false \rBrack \triangleq \emptyset \lBrack t \rBrack \triangleq \{ (i, \alpha ) \mid i \in I, t \in \alpha \} \lBrack b \lor c \rBrack \triangleq \lBrack b \rBrack \cup \lBrack c \rBrack \lBrack \true \rBrack \triangleq I \times \At \lBrack x = i \rBrack \triangleq \{ (i, \alpha ) \mid \alpha \in \At \} \lBrack b \land c \rBrack \triangleq \lBrack b \rBrack \cap \lBrack c \rBrack \lBrack \neg b \rBrack \triangleq I \times \At \setminus \lBrack b \rBrack\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-009: \begin{equation}\lBrack \false \rBrack \triangleq \emptyset \lBrack t \rBrack \triangleq \{ (i, \alpha ) \mid i \in I, t \in \alpha \} \lBrack b \lor c \rBrack \triangleq \lBrack b \rBrack \cup \lBrack c \rBrack \lBrack \true \rBrack \triangleq I \times \At \lBrack x = i \rBrack \triangleq \{ (i, \alpha ) \mid \alpha \in \At \} \lBrack b \land c \rBrack \triangleq \lBrack b \rBrack \cap \lBrack c \rBrack \lBrack \neg b \rBrack \triangleq I \times \At \setminus \lBrack b \rBrack\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-085: Take $T = \{ t_1, t_2 \}$ and $I = \{ 1, 2, 3 \}$; then we can calculate that \begin{equation}\lBrack (t_1 \lor \neg t_2) \land (x = 2) \rBrack = \{ (\{ t_1, t_2\}, 2), (\{ t_1 \}, 2), ( \emptyset , 2) \}\end{equation} In other words, the test above holds in execution contexts where the indicator has value $2$, and either $t_1$ and $t_2$ are both true (first element), both false (last element), or where $t_{1}$ is true but $t_{2}$ is false (middle element).
elements.sent-2411-086: In contrast, $ \lBrack x = 1 \land x = 3 \rBrack = \emptyset $, which is to say that this test does not hold in any execution context, because the indicator variable \(x\) cannot be both 1 and 3 at the same time.
elements.sent-2411-087: We can now turn our attention to the semantics of CF-GKAT\@.
elements.sent-2411-088: Like (G)KAT, the semantics of CF-GKAT is given in terms of guarded languages~\cite{smolka_GuardedKleeneAlgebra_2020,kozen_KleeneAlgebraTests_1997 , which are best thought of as sets of symbolic traces of the program.}
elements.sent-2411-089: These traces record the initial, intermediate and final machine states observed during execution, as well as the actions that occurred between those states.
elements.sent-2411-090: Because the value of the indicator variable matters only for control flow, we do not consider indicators to be part of the machine state; hence, machine states in a guarded word are drawn from $\At$.
elements.sent-2411-091: A guarded word is a sequence of the form $ \alpha _{1 p_{1 \alpha _{2 p_{2 \cdots \alpha _{n-1 p_{n \alpha _{n}$, where \( \alpha _{i \in \At\) and \(p_{i \in \Sigma \); that is to say, guarded words are elements of the regular language $\At \cdot ( \Sigma \cdot \At)^*$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-092: We refer to sets of guarded words as guarded languages; the set of guarded languages is denoted $\mathcal{G}$.
elements.sent-2411-094: Now the guarded word $\{ t_{1 \}p_{1}\{ t_2 \}p_{2}\emptyset$ represents a program trace that starts out in a machine state where $t_1$ is true (but $t_{2}$ is not).}
elements.sent-2411-095: The program then executes the action $p_{1}$, after which $t_2$ is true (but $t_{1}$ is not).
elements.sent-2411-096: Finally, the program goes on to execute the action $p_{2}$, and halts in a state where neither $t_{1}$ nor $t_{2}$ is true.
elements.sent-2411-097: Our semantics of a CF-GKAT expression will ultimately be a guarded language.
elements.sent-2411-098: However, due to the non-local nature of our language, we cannot compute this semantics inductively, as the label in \command{goto may occur in a different part of the program whose traces have not yet been computed.}
elements.sent-2411-099: To address this challenge, we introduce an intermediate continuation semantics, which accounts for both the indicator variables and the non-local control flow.
elements.sent-2411-100: The remainder of this subsection explains the domain of continuation semantics, based on guarded words with continuations.
elements.sent-2411-101: Intuitively, these are guarded words equipped with a piece of information called a continuation, which indicates how control flow continues after the program ends.
elements.sent-2411-102: The inclusion continuation information at the end of a trace allows us to define a semantics of CF-GKAT expressions inductively.
elements.sent-2411-103: Once the continuation semantics of a CF-GKAT program is known, we can flatten it into a guarded language.
elements.sent-2411-104: A guarded word with continuation is a pair $w \cdot c$, where $w$ is a guarded word and $c$ is a continuation, which can take on one of the following forms for $i \in I$ and $ \ell \in L$: \begin{mathpar \acc{i \and \brk{i \and \ret \and \jmp{( \ell , i) \end{mathpar We write $C$ for the set of all continuations.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-105: A set of guarded words with continuations is a guarded language with continuations; the set of guarded languages with continuations is written $\mathcal{C}$.
elements.sent-2411-106: Intuitively, the different types of continuation may be interpreted as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-107: The continuation $\acc{i}$ represents that the trace has successfully reached the end of this part of the program, with indicator value \(i\).
elements.sent-2411-108: Execution can be picked up if the program is put in a larger context --- e.g., if $w \cdot \acc{i}$ is a trace of $e$, then it may be combined with a trace found when $f$ is executed with indicator value $i$ to compute the semantics of $e; f$.
elements.sent-2411-109: A continuation of the form $\brk{i}$ signals that the trace ends by halting the loop in which it occurs.
elements.sent-2411-110: Execution can resume only after this loop (with indicator value $i$).
elements.sent-2411-111: This kind of trace cannot be composed on the right, as is done for traces with accepting continuations, because we first need to enclose it in a loop to halt; it will then be converted into $\acc{i}$.
elements.sent-2411-112: The continuation $\ret$ represents a trace that ends in the program halting completely.
elements.sent-2411-113: Traces of this kind will percolate upwards in the semantics, without changing their continuation.
elements.sent-2411-114: These are intended to model the $\comRet$ statement, which halts the program no matter how deeply it is nested.
elements.sent-2411-115: In this case, the indicator value does not matter any more.
elements.sent-2411-116: Finally, the continuation $\jmp{(\ell, i)}$ is put on traces that will continue executing from label $\ell$, with indicator value $i$.
elements.sent-2411-117: Like $\brk{i}$ and $\ret$, these traces do not compose on the right, but unlike $\brk{i}$ this continuation does not change, as jump resolution happens only at the end, when the continuation semantics is known for the entire program.
elements.sent-2411-118: Let $w$ be the guarded word from the previous example; the guarded word with continuation $w \cdot \jmp{( \ell _{1 , 2)}$ represents a partial program trace that takes the steps in $w$, continues executing at the label $ \ell _{1}$ with indicator value $2$.}
elements.sent-2411-119: More examples appear in the next section.
elements.sent-2411-120: The continuation semantics of a CF-GKAT expression takes a starting indicator value, and produces a guarded language with continuations representing the traces of that program when started with the given indicator value.
elements.sent-2411-121: To properly encode this, we need the following notion.
elements.sent-2411-122: An indexed family of guarded languages (with continuations), or ``indexed family'' for the sake of brevity, is function from $I$ to guarded languages (with continuations).
elements.sent-2411-123: Typically, we use \(G\) and \(H\) to denote an indexed family.
elements.sent-2411-124: To lighten notation, we write \(G_{i}\) to denote \(G(i)\).
elements.sent-2411-125: Similar to guarded languages, indexed families can be composed in several ways.
elements.sent-2411-126: In particular, we are interested in the sequencing operation and the Kleene star operation of indexed families, because these will turn out to be useful when defining the continuation semantics of CF-GKAT\@.
elements.sent-2411-127: When sequencing two families \(G\) and \(H\), the traces in \(G_i\) with a continuation of the form $\acc{j}$ will be composed with traces in \(H_j\); traces with different continuations are copied over in full, because they do not compose on the right.
elements.sent-2411-128: Formally, this operation is defined as follows.
elements.sent-2411-130: We write $G \diamond H$ for the sequencing (or concatenation) operation of \(G\) and \(H\), which is defined as the smallest family of guarded languages with continuations (in the pointwise order) satisfying the following rules for all $i,j \in I$ as well as all $ \ell \in L$: \begin{mathpar \inferrule{ w \alpha \cdot \acc{j \in G_{i \alpha x \cdot c \in H_{j { w \alpha x \cdot c \in (G \diamond H)_{i \and \inferrule{ w \cdot c \in G_{j c \in \{ \brk{i , \acc{i , \jmp{(\ell, i) \} { w \cdot c \in (G \diamond H)_{j \end{mathpar.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-131: The first rule composes accepting traces in $G$ with traces in $H$, picking up with the indicator value where the first trace left off.
elements.sent-2411-132: Note also that this rule requires the last atom in the trace on the left to match the first atom in the trace on the right, because we want the second trace to start from the machine state computed in the first trace.
elements.sent-2411-133: This mirrors the coalesced product used to define the sequential composition of guarded languages (without continuations)~\cite{kozen_KleeneAlgebraTests_1997 .}
elements.sent-2411-134: The last rule ensures that traces that encountered non-local control flow within $G$ are preserved in $G \diamond H$.

elements.sent-2411-222: The transition map \( \delta \) assigns every state a dynamics, while the jump map $ \lambda $ assigns a dynamics to each label, indicating how to resume computation when a jump continuation is reached.
elements.sent-2411-224: The behavior of this automaton, when starting from \(\hat{s}\), is as follows.

elements.sent-2411-325: We summarize our decision procedure as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-326: Given two CF-GKAT programs \(e, f\), we first collect their alphabets $\Sigma$, $T$, $I$, and $L$.
elements.sent-2411-327: We gather the sets of primitive actions \(\Sigma\), primitive tests \(T\), and labels \(L\) that are present in either \(e\) or \(f\).
elements.sent-2411-328: Additionally, we identify the set of indicator values \(I\), encompassing those found in either \(e\) or \(f\), along with an additional indicator \(*\) that is exclusive to both programs.
elements.sent-2411-329: We then proceed to compute the Thompson automata of \(e\) and \(f\) and convert them into CF-GKAT automata, denoted as \(A_e\) and \(A_f\).
elements.sent-2411-330: It is noteworthy that these automata preserve the continuation semantics (\Cref{the:thompson-correctness ).}
elements.sent-2411-332: Subsequently, we lower both \(A_e\) and \(A_f\) to GKAT automata \(A_e\! \downarrow _{i}\) and \(A_f\! \downarrow _{i}\) for each \(i \in I\).

elements.sent-2411-338: We now give a rough account of the computational cost for deciding equivalence of CF-GKAT programs.
elements.sent-2411-339: Like GKAT, we consider $T$ to be fixed for the purpose of analyzing complexity; otherwise, deciding equivalence of (CF-)GKAT programs is co-NP hard~\cite{smolka_GuardedKleeneAlgebra_2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-340: Starting with an expression \(e\), we observe that the number of states in the Thompson automaton \(A_e\) is bounded by \(|e|\), which is the size of $e$ as a term.
elements.sent-2411-341: While computing this automaton we must bear in mind that deriving the iterated start dynamics of a loop using $\iter$ may take up to $|I|$ steps (assuming we use memoization to compute the output for each input), and this happens at most $|e|$ times.
elements.sent-2411-342: After lowering, each GKAT automaton \(A_e\! \downarrow \) contains at most \(|I| \times |e|\) states, and computing the jump resolution using $\iter$ takes on the order of $|L| \times |I|$ steps (again using memoization).
elements.sent-2411-343: For each \(i \in I\), determining the equivalence between \(A_e\! \downarrow _{i}\) and \(A_f\! \downarrow _{i}\) is accomplished in (nearly\footnote{We omit the factor coming from the inverse Ackermann function $\hat{\alpha (n)$, which is at most $5$ for any realistic number of states, out of consideration for the sake of simplicity.}}
elements.sent-2411-344: To verify trace equivalence between \(e\) and \(f\), this check is required for \(A_e\! \downarrow _{i}\) and \(A_f\! \downarrow _{i}\) across all \(i \in I\).
elements.sent-2411-345: Therefore, the overall time spent on the equivalence checks is on the order of \(|I|^2 \times (|e|+|f|)\), while computing the automata can take roughly $|L| \times |I| + |I| \times (|e| + |f|)$ time.
elements.sent-2411-346: This implies that the algorithm's complexity scales (nearly) linearly with the sizes of \(e\) and \(f\), linearly in the size of $L$, and quadratically with respect to the number of indicator values \(|I|\).
elements.sent-2411-347: We hypothesize that CF-GKAT can be a useful tool to check whether two programs have the same control flow --- that is, under the same circumstances, they execute the same sequence of actions.
elements.sent-2411-348: In this section, we report on the performance of CF-GKAT in two particular use cases.
elements.sent-2411-349: The first use case concerns the control flow structuring stage of a decompiler.
elements.sent-2411-350: Briefly put, a decompiler is a program that infers a high-level language representation of a binary executable file.
elements.sent-2411-351: In earlier stages, the decompiler builds a control-flow graph from the binary~\cite{DBLP:conf/pldi/VerbeekBFR22 , which represents how control transfers from one part to the next.}
elements.sent-2411-352: The control flow structuring pass is tasked with inferring an equivalent representation of this control flow in terms of constructs like \command{if-then-else and \command{while-do .}}
elements.sent-2411-353: The second use case is about refactoring operations aimed at making code more readable by eliminating $\comGoto$ instructions, which may introduce indicator variables~\cite{erosa-hendren-1994,DBLP:journals/tsi/CasseFRS02 .}
elements.sent-2411-354: CF-GKAT can be used to validate that the control flow of the input (graph or code) corresponds to that of the output code.
elements.sent-2411-355: In general, such algorithms can never be complete with respect to input-output equivalences, as they cannot automatically validate the correctness of refactorings depend on the meaning of primitive commands.
elements.sent-2411-356: However, CF-GKAT should be applicable to refactoring operations that rearrange the code for the sake of improving the presentation of the control flow.
elements.sent-2411-357: We target the C language as it is a widely used programming language that allows for non-local control flow.
elements.sent-2411-358: Our implementation is written in OCaml and is divided into a front-end and a back-end~\cite{artifact .}
elements.sent-2411-359: The front-end converts a function defined in a C file to a CF-GKAT expression, using the \command{clangML}\footnote{\url{https://memcad.gitlabpages.inria.fr/clangml/ OCaml bindings for \command{clang .\footnote{\url{https://clang.llvm.org/ The conversion first determines which variables, if any, qualify as indicator variables and picks (at most) one.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-360: Next, it lifts the C syntax tree to a CF-GKAT program, mapping (1)~all assignments and comparisons of the indicator variable to their CF-GKAT counterparts, (2)~all other primitive statements to uninterpreted actions, and (3)~all control flow constructs to the corresponding CF-GKAT operator whenever possible.
elements.sent-2411-361: The back-end is responsible for compiling a CF-GKAT expression to a GKAT automaton, and for comparing two GKAT automata.
elements.sent-2411-362: This GKAT automaton is derived using the construction from \Cref{section:decision procedure ; equivalence is decided by checking bisimulation~\cite{smolka_GuardedKleeneAlgebra_2020 .}}
elements.sent-2411-363: These parts combine into a tool that accepts two C files, and checks whether their functions (paired by name) have the same control flow.
elements.sent-2411-364: Not all C programs can be converted to CF-KAT expressions, and so our front-end is not complete; instead, we aimed to support a sizeable portion of the code in GNU \command{coreutils to perform the experiments described in the next section.}
elements.sent-2411-365: In particular, our front-end internally converts \command{for -loops to \command{while -loops, and \command{do-while loops to (partially unrolled) \command{while loops.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-366: The former transformation is sound; the latter is admissible when the loop body does not contain \command{break or a label, which is the case for our experiment below.}
elements.sent-2411-367: We used GNU \command{coreutils as a source of C code containing non-local control flow.}
elements.sent-2411-368: As is customary with projects of this size, the code is organized into several files that collectively define hundreds of functions, ranging from very simple to very complex.

elements.sent-2411-408: Because we anticipate that the primary use case of CF-GKAT is the verification and validation of (de)compilers, we briefly go over existing work in this area, and relate it to ours.
elements.sent-2411-409: Compiler verification and validation is a well-developed research area.
elements.sent-2411-410: The most rigorous approach is to use a proof assistant to verify the compilation algorithm relative to a formal semantics of the source and target language~\cite{leroy_CompCertFormallyVerified_2016,tan-etal-2016,neis-etal-2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-411: This method offers a foundational guarantee of correctness without imposing runtime overhead, but can be challenging and time-consuming to adapt on a large scale.
elements.sent-2411-412: We envision that CF-GKAT could be used to automate correctness proofs of program transformations (provided that CF-GKAT equivalence is sound w.r.t.\ the language semantics).
elements.sent-2411-413: Compiler testing feeds synthesized programs into different or differently configured compilers, and compares the resulting programs~\cite{chen_SurveyCompilerTesting_2021a,le_CompilerValidationEquivalence_2014a,yang_FindingUnderstandingBugs_2011 .}
elements.sent-2411-414: While this approach does not provide any formal guarantee of correctness, it has been effective in finding compiler bugs~\cite{yang_FindingUnderstandingBugs_2011 .}
elements.sent-2411-415: Within this framework, CF-GKAT could be used as an equivalence checking oracle; however, because our work is limited to control flow equivalence, a blinding pass (similar to our experiments above) is likely necessary.
elements.sent-2411-416: Translation validation~\cite{goldberg_LoopsPracticalIssues_2005,kasampalis_LanguageparametricCompilerValidation_2021,necula_TranslationValidationOptimizing_2000,kasampalis_TranslationValidationCompilation_2021,zhang_FormalVerificationOptimizing_2018,pnueli-etal-1998,sewell-etal-2013 scales well and offers formal guarantees.}
elements.sent-2411-417: This is achieved through the use of symbolic evaluations and bisimulation to generate verification conditions for SMT solvers, allowing the validator to handle both data-flow and control-flow transformations simultaneously, albeit at the cost of decidability.
elements.sent-2411-418: Some approaches also employ graph isomorphism checks~\cite{awadhutkar-etal-2022 , which can be more computationally expensive than bisimulation.}
elements.sent-2411-419: Compared to other efforts, our focus is exclusively on control flow transformations.
elements.sent-2411-420: This allows us to work with a minimal language, sufficient to cover well-known control-flow transformations~\cite{yakdan_NoMoreGotos_2015,erosa-hendren-1994,DBLP:journals/cacm/BohmJ66,grathwohl_KAT_2014 , and establish meta-properties such as correctness of our decision procedure and low complexity --- all of which are properties out of reach for unabstracted (Turing-complete) languages.}
elements.sent-2411-421: Predicate abstraction~\cite{ball-etal-2001 can help focus on relevant parts of a program prior to analysis, which makes it similar to blinding.}
elements.sent-2411-422: However, predicate abstraction requires input on how to abstract the program, whereas blinding abstracts away all parts that are not relevant to flow control.
elements.sent-2411-423: Decompiler verification and validation is, by comparison, not as explored as compiler verifcation and validation.

elements.sent-2411-426: There has been some preliminary work on developing a sound decompiler that can produce C code, although control flow structuring is limited~\cite{DBLP:conf/sefm/VerbeekOR20 .}

elements.subsection-2411-015: Kleene Algebra And Control Flow Verification
elements.sent-2411-428: Existing work has explored non-local control-flow structures and indicator variables within the framework of KAT, albeit with a number of differences from our work.
elements.sent-2411-429: Kozen characterized the semantics for programs with non-local control-flow structures as a family of KAT expressions~\cite{kozen_NonlocalFlowControl_2008a .}
elements.sent-2411-430: This approach yields a decision procedure for program equivalences.
elements.sent-2411-431: In contrast, CF-GKAT takes a more explicit approach by defining the continuation semantics, and the equivalence is computed by converting programs directly into automata.
elements.sent-2411-432: This resolves an open question by \citet{kozen_NonlocalFlowControl_2008a , on whether non-local control flow structures can be treated ``directly'', without being converted into KAT expressions.}
elements.sent-2411-433: The interested reader is referred to~\cite{tencate-kappe-2024 for an investigation of the limits of compositionally specified deterministic control flow.}
elements.sent-2411-434: Concretely, their setter \(b!\) equates to indicator assignment \(x = \true\) and \(\overline{b !\) to \(x = \false\); similarly, their tester \(b?\) corresponds to primitive indicator tests.}
elements.sent-2411-435: Although this is a special case of indicator variables, KAT+B!\@ can simulate indicator variable over a finite set \(I\) with \(|I|\) mutable tests.
elements.sent-2411-436: We opt to treat indicator assignments and tests as primitives, rather than restricting ourselves to (boolean-valued) mutable tests.
elements.sent-2411-437: Our treatment of indicator variables also draws inspiration from NetKAT~\cite{anderson_NetKATSemanticFoundations_2014a .}
elements.sent-2411-438: Specifically, NetKAT can be seen as a special case of KAT with indicator variables, the only primitive action is dup.
elements.sent-2411-439: KATch~\cite{moeller-etal-2024 , a fast symbolic equivalence checker for NetKAT, is of particular interest to us in this space.}
elements.sent-2411-440: In the future, we hope to scale our equivalence checker using similar symbolic techniques.
elements.sent-2411-441: To reason about variable assignment beyond indicator variables, Schematic KAT~\cite{angus_KleeneAlgebraTests_2001 provides a fine-grained algebraic theory for assignments over uninterpreted functions.}
elements.sent-2411-442: Later, Schematic KAT was also extended to reason about local variables~\cite{aboul-hosn_LocalVariableScoping_2008a .}
elements.sent-2411-443: Neither work covers the complexity of the equivalence problem for schematic KAT and its extensions.
elements.sent-2411-444: Kleene algebra can also be extended with nominal techniques~\cite{kozen_CompletenessIncompletenessNominal_2015,kozen_NominalKleeneCoalgebra_2015,gabbay_FreshnessNameRestrictionSets_2011 , which may help to reason about potentially infinite data domains, although the inclusion of tests to nominal Kleene algebra has not yet been investigated.}
elements.sent-2411-445: Separately, \citet{kozen_CertificationCompilerOptimizations_2000b have applied KAT to verify compiler correctness.}
elements.sent-2411-446: Their system directly uses postulated equalities for parts of their verification task.
elements.sent-2411-447: In contrast, our framework is based on the trace semantics, a commonly accepted semantics for \command{while -programs.}
elements.sent-2411-448: Finally, unlike the systems above, our system is based on GKAT, instead of KAT\@.
elements.sent-2411-449: This enhances the scalability of our equivalence checking algorithms to accommodate larger programs: whereas equivalence of KAT expressions is PSPACE-complete~\cite{cohen-kozen-smith-1996 , equivalence of CF-GKAT expressions can be verified in polynomial time for a fixed number of tests~\cite{smolka_GuardedKleeneAlgebra_2020 .}}
elements.sent-2411-450: Symbolic techniques previously applied to (Net)KAT may also provide better ways of mitigating the complexity of tests~\cite{pous-2015,moeller-etal-2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-451: Our system is the first to integrate both indicator variables and non-local control flow in a unified framework, which enables the verification of complex control-flow transformations that leverage both indicator variables and non-local control flow~\cite{yakdan_NoMoreGotos_2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-452: Our notion of trace equivalence is also coarser than previous systems; CF-GKAT equates programs ending in different indicator values: \begin{equation}{c} x := \true; \comITE{x = \true}{\command{print(1)}}{\command{print(2)}} [1mm] x := \false; \comITE{x = \false}{\command{print(1)}}{\command{print(2)}} \end{equation} The two programs above are equivalent to an outside observer, as both of them will print 1; yet the assignment of \(x\) is different at the end of the so, thus systems like KAT+B!~\cite{grathwohl_KAT_2014 will not equate them.}
elements.sent-2411-453: On the other hand, our equivalence is not a congruence.
elements.sent-2411-454: For example, concatenating $\comAssert{(x = true)}$ to the two equivalent programs above will yield inequivalent programs.
elements.sent-2411-455: In this paper, we introduced CF-GKAT (Control-Flow GKAT), a system that extends GKAT with non-local control flow and indicator variables to validate control-flow transformation programs.
elements.sent-2411-456: We formalized two semantics for CF-GKAT\@.
elements.sent-2411-457: The first is the continuation semantics, where each trace, represented as a guarded word, is augmented with a continuation.
elements.sent-2411-458: The second is the trace semantics, which is obtained by resolving the continuations in the continuation semantics.
elements.sent-2411-459: We proposed CF-GKAT automata as the operational model for CF-GKAT programs, and showed how to obtain these automata from CF-GKAT expressions while preserving their continuation semantics.

elements.sent-2411-485: CCN alone is not the discriminating factor that prevents us from blinding functions: a run of Lizard on the original source code of Coreutils shows that, while we are unable to blind functions with very high CCN (the maximum being 281), the median and mode CCN of functions we are not able to blind are 9 and 2, respectively, which shows there are relatively simple functions with respect to the CCN metric that we cannot handle.
